Arguments.prompt: Download US state and county boundary shapefiles from the Census Bureau (states: https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_state_20m.zip, counties: https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_county_5m.zip). Filter to the state named Oregon and the county named Benton that lies within that state. Compute an outer bounding box around the county by expanding its min/max latitude and longitude by 0.5 degrees in each direction. Download the NOAA GHCND station inventory from https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/pub/data/ghcn/daily/ghcnd-inventory.txt, keep only stations that have both TMAX and TMIN records with an end date in the current year or later, and filter those stations to only the ones that fall within the outer bounding box. Save four GeoJSON files: State.geojson, County.geojson, OuterBoundary.geojson, and Stations.geojson. Upload all four files to the data store under the folder WeatherGeographicPlot.

Download Stations.geojson from the data store folder WeatherGeographicPlot. For each station, download its full daily CSV record from https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/data/global-historical-climatology-network-daily/access/<STATION_ID>.csv. Determine the target week: take today's date, subtract 28 days, then back up to the most recent Monday — use that Monday as the week start and the following Sunday as the week end. For each station CSV, filter rows to that date range, keeping TMIN and TMAX columns. Discard any rows with missing values. Average TMIN and TMAX separately across all days per station, then divide both averages by 10 to convert from tenths-of-a-degree to whole degrees Celsius. Combine all stations into a single GeoDataFrame with columns STATION, geometry, TMIN, and TMAX. Save the result as TemperatureData.geojson and upload it to the data store under folder WeatherGeographicPlot.

Download OuterBoundary.geojson, TemperatureData.geojson, State.geojson, and County.geojson from the data store folder WeatherGeographicPlot. Create a figure with two side-by-side subplots (12x6 inches). For each subplot, build a 100x100 interpolation grid spanning the outer boundary, then use cubic interpolation (scipy.interpolate.griddata) to interpolate station TMIN values (left subplot) and TMAX values (right subplot) across that grid. Render each as a filled contour plot (20 levels) with alpha 0.8 — use colormap 'Blues_r' for TMIN and 'Reds' for TMAX. Overlay the station points as scatter markers colored by their value, with black edges. Draw the state and county boundaries on both subplots. Set axis limits to the outer boundary extents, match the aspect ratio to the county bounds, and set tick marks every 0.5 degrees. Label axes as Longitude and Latitude, add a colorbar labeled 'Temperature (°C)', and title each subplot 'Minimum Temperature Heatmap (°C)' and 'Maximum Temperature Heatmap (°C)'. Use the invocation ID (formatted as %Y-%m-%d-%H-%M-%S) to determine the week start date (subtract 28 days then back up to Monday) and set the figure supertitle to 'Temperature Heatmap for Benton County for week starting <day>, <month> <date>, <year>'. Save the figure as TemperatureHeatmap.png and upload it to the data store under folder WeatherGeographicPlot.
